title: Technology | Fragomen, Del Rey, Bernsen & Loewy LLP
content:
Start with the rapidly changing demographics of the high-tech workforce. Add the unpredictable churn of national immigration politics. Stir in disruption caused by a global health crisis.
What do you have? The most difficult mobility environment in the history of the technology industry.
An effective mobile workforce is part of any tech company’s DNA. And now more than ever, smart, business-savvy immigration guidance is key to its future.
Through a unique global perspective, Fragomen is intimately familiar with the forces roiling the global technology industry, from the emergence of India and China as epochal sources of technical talent to a new multi-jurisdictional, global approach to employee placement no longer centered primarily on the United States.
We work tirelessly to create practical solutions for our clients. Solutions that help manage an unpredictable and often frustrating mobility environment—and identify and develop new opportunities and relationships as well.
Clients rely on us for our:
The vagaries of politics, combined with the severe travel restrictions provoked by COVID-19, have inspired companies to seek immigration-friendly opportunities outside of the United States, especially in the European, Asia-Pacific and Southeast Asia markets.
As a global service provider, Fragomen assists companies with immigration needs that cross every conceivable combination of markets and jurisdictions; in the process, we transform immigration from a fact of life into a valued component of global business strategy.

### Solution-driven mindset
Creativity and flexibility are the order of the day as COVID continues to wreak havoc on world travel. Technology companies need an immigration partner that can roll with the punches and offer workable alternatives alongside standard solutions. Our up-to-the-second understanding of worldwide COVID travel restrictions is unparalleled.
Since the beginning of the pandemic, Fragomen has identified effective strategies to improve successful outcomes, including critical company and national interest exemptions, “green channel” exemptions in China, and remote work provisions. We also help clients match employees who are unable to remain in the United States with placement in alternative jurisdictions until a new visa or other arrangements can be secured.

### History in the field
Technology evolves in the blink of an eye, but our relationships with our technology industry clients endure. We’ve partnered with global technology companies, from the biggest players to startups on the cutting edge, for nearly forty years. Our services have grown and evolved with the industry and, as a result, our familiarity with the sector is unmatched.

### Commitment to innovation
We’ve evolved alongside our technology clients, possessed by an unrelenting spirit of technology innovation. From our Immigration Technology Lab , we focus on pioneering sophisticated solutions that revolutionize the immigration experience for our clients. We’re flexible and adaptable—we speak our clients’ language.
We understand how the frustration and unpredictability of immigration directly affects the lives of real people, their families and their loved ones. Ensuring a positive and comfortable employee experience has been central to our success as a firm. Many of our professionals are migrants too—we know what our clients are going through. This empathy has never been more important to our clients than it is now.

## Explore more at Fragomen
Fragomen earned top national and regional rankings in Chambers USA 2026, including its 13th consecutive year in Band 1 nationwide.
Senior Manager Samantha Arnold examines the remaining opportunities under Ireland’s winding-down Immigrant Investor Programme and what they mean for eligible investors and their families.
Government Affairs Strategy Director Shuyeb Muquit analyses the UK’s latest net migration decline, exploring what the data reveals about policy impact, economic trade‑offs and the risks of over‑correction in future migration strategy.
Partner Bo Cooper discusses the practical considerations facing employers and foreign nationals amid recent changes to green card processing.
In this #FragomenFC episode, Partner Rick Lamanna, Senior Manager Sergio Flores and Senior Associate Jake Paul Minster discuss final travel and immigration considerations ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
Senior Manager Samantha Arnold discusses Ireland’s citizenship framework and the gaps that can remain following reforms to birthright citizenship.
In this video, Partner Diana Quintas discusses how Brazil’s family reunion visa supports long-term international assignments and outlines key eligibility, documentation and compliance considerations for employers and families relocating to Brazil.
Attorney María José Clarke explores Chile’s intensified immigration enforcement framework under President José Antonio Kast, examining new employer compliance obligations, work authorization rules for foreign nationals and the legal and financial consequences of unauthorized employment.
Senior Manager Jonathan Hill examines how the UK’s new RAG rating system and stricter compliance metrics could shape universities’ approaches to international student recruitment and sponsorship compliance.
Knowledge Management Director Ana Sofia Walsh and Senior Client Engagement Manager Soraya Driessen examine the European Commission’s EU Inc. proposal and its potential to reshape corporate structures across the EU, highlighting the resulting complexities and unresolved questions for immigration, work authorization and cross-border workforce mobility.
